question: [File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: extractCryptoAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the 'ne' (not-equal) operator is explicitly blocked for spender.id with an InvalidArgumentError, but all other operators (eq, lt, lte, gt, gte) are allowed, to craft a range query (spender.id=gte:1&spender.id=lte:999999999) that returns all allowances on a staking reward account in a single paginated sweep?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that EntityService.getEncodedId() does not verify that the resolved account is of type ACCOUNT (vs CONTRACT, FILE, TOKEN, etc.) before querying allowances, allowing the attacker to query allowances on a contract account that holds staking rewards, discovering spenders who can drain those rewards?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user use the spender field returned in the allowances response to identify their own account as a spender on a victim's account, confirm the allowance amount is sufficient to cover the victim's pending staking rewards, and then execute a CryptoTransfer using that allowance to steal the rewards before the victim claims them?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: extractCryptoAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with limit=1 and repeatedly use the pagination next link (which encodes the last spender ID) to walk through all allowances one by one on a staking reward account, avoiding any bulk-query detection while still enumerating all spenders who could drain unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the ownerCondition uses a static string 'owner = $1' without any account type validation, allowing them to query allowances for system-level accounts (e.g., 0.0.98 treasury, 0.0.800 staking rewards) that may have granted allowances to other accounts, enabling theft of unpaid staking reward funds?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of rate limiting or authentication on the allowances endpoint to perform a mass enumeration of all accounts in the network, identifying which accounts have active crypto allowances with large amounts that correspond to accounts with high pending staking rewards, building a target list for staking reward theft?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: extractCryptoAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a spender.id=eq: filter with a value equal to the Hedera staking reward account (0.0.800) to check if any account has granted an allowance to the staking reward account itself, which if true would allow the staking reward account to be used as a proxy to redirect reward payments to the attacker?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user use the amount_granted field exposed by BaseAllowanceViewModel to identify allowances where amount_granted is very large (e.g., MAX_LONG), indicating an unlimited allowance on a staking reward account, and then use that allowance to drain the entire account balance including all accumulated unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user pass an EVM address in the path parameter that corresponds to a long-zero address (0x000000000000000000000000000000000000000320 for account 0.0.800) to EntityService.getEncodedId(), which resolves it via getEntityIdFromEvmAddress(), and then enumerate allowances on the staking reward account to identify theft vectors?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: extractCryptoAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the parameter position calculation in updateConditionsAndParamsWithInValues() where position = conditions.length + 1 (not params.length + 1), and since conditions starts with ownerCondition (length=1), the first non-eq spender filter gets position=2, but if the attacker sends two non-eq spender filters, the second gets position=3 while params only has 2 entries, causing a query error that leaks database schema information useful for planning staking reward theft?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the response includes the full owner account ID in EntityId.parse(baseAllowance.owner).toString() format, allowing them to cross-reference the owner with public staking data to identify accounts with large pending staking rewards that have active allowances, prioritizing those accounts for theft?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that EntityService.getEncodedId() throws NotFoundError (not InvalidArgumentError) when an EVM address is not found, while throwing InvalidArgumentError for malformed inputs, allowing the attacker to use the error type as an oracle to enumerate valid staking account EVM addresses without triggering input validation errors?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: extractCryptoAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with order=desc (the default) and no spender filter to retrieve allowances in descending spender order, where the highest spender IDs are likely recently created accounts that may have been granted allowances specifically to drain staking rewards, enabling the attacker to identify the most recent and potentially most dangerous allowances?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CryptoAllowance model's Object.assign with mapKeys(camelCase) constructor to discover that if the database returns unexpected columns (e.g., from a schema migration), those columns are automatically included in the model object and potentially leaked through the ViewModel, exposing sensitive staking reward account metadata?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user use the allowances endpoint to confirm whether they themselves are listed as a spender on a target account by querying /accounts/{victim}/allowances/crypto?spender.id={attacker}, and if the response shows a non-zero amount, use that allowance to drain the victim's HBAR balance including unpaid staking rewards before the victim revokes the allowance?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: extractCryptoAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the amountCondition ('amount > 0') is a static string appended to conditions without parameterization, and if the database uses a non-standard type for the amount column (e.g., numeric vs bigint), could a type coercion edge case cause amount=0 rows to satisfy amount > 0, exposing revoked allowances on staking reward accounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the pagination link generation (utils.getPaginationLink with lastValues = {spender.id: lastRow.spender}) to inject a crafted spender value into the next page URL, causing the subsequent request to use a manipulated spender.id filter that bypasses the amount > 0 condition and returns zero-amount allowances on staking reward accounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user query allowances for a victim account, observe the timestamp.from field to determine when the allowance was last modified, and use this timing information to infer the victim's staking reward accumulation period, enabling the attacker to calculate the approximate unpaid staking reward balance and decide whether it is worth executing a theft?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: extractCryptoAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with multiple spender.id=eq: filters for the same spender ID, causing spenderInValues to contain duplicates, which then generates a SQL IN clause with duplicate values (e.g., 'spender in ($2, $2)'), potentially causing unexpected query behavior that bypasses the amount > 0 filter and exposes zero-amount allowances on staking reward accounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that EntityService.getEncodedId() accepts both shard.realm.num format and encoded numeric IDs, allowing the attacker to query allowances using the encoded numeric ID of a staking reward account (which may differ from the human-readable 0.0.800 format) to bypass any input validation that only checks for the human-readable format?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user use the allowances API to perform reconnaissance on a victim account by querying allowances with different spender.id ranges, building a complete picture of all authorized spenders, their allowance amounts, and grant timestamps, then use this information to impersonate a legitimate spender and steal unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: extractCryptoAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the default order is DESC (orderFilterValues.DESC) and the default limit comes from getResponseLimit(), by sending a request with no parameters to get the most recently modified allowances first, identifying freshly granted allowances on accounts that just received staking rewards and are therefore prime targets for theft?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/cryptoAllowanceController.js -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the allowances endpoint does not validate whether the queried account is a staking participant, allowing the attacker to query allowances for any account including staking node accounts (0.0.3-0.0.28) that accumulate rewards, discovering which accounts have been granted spending authority over those reward funds?
